title: Dha Werda Verda
content:
{{Top|leg|italics=1}} {{InUniverseMedia |image= |name=''Dha Werda Verda'' |author=[[Taung/Legends|Taungs]]{{SW|url=news/star-wars-the-essential-guide-to-warfare-authors-cut-part-2-ancient-coruscant|series=Star Wars: The Essential Guide to Warfare Author's Cut|text=Part 2 - Ancient Coruscant}} |editor= |illustrator= |artist= |performer=*[[Mandalorian/Legends|Mandalorians]] *[[Clone trooper/Legends|Clone troopers]] and [[Clone commando/Legends|commandos]]''[[Republic Commando: Triple Zero]]'' |publisher= |release date= |media type=*Poem *[[Music/Legends|Chant]] and ritual dance |pages= |published in= |genre=*Historical epic *War chant }} {{Quote|When the dawn came the Zhell awakened and saw the Taungs upon the high place and were afraid, for the morning light caught the glint of helms and weapons and created phantom warriors, made of dazzle and distance. But the cleverest of them were not deceived, and saw how few we were. And so they assembled without haste, merry in mockery, and prepared to march. And in the high place we awaited death.|Translated excerpt from the ninth chapter of the ''Dha Werda Verda'' epic|''[[The Essential Guide to Warfare]]''}} '''''Dha Werda Verda''''' was the name of both an epic poem, and a [[Mandalorian/Legends|Mandalorian]] war [[Music/Legends|chant]], as well as the [[Taung/Legends|Taung]] [[Species/Legends|people]] that lay at the [[Heart/Legends|heart]] of each. When translated from the [[Language/Legends|language]] of [[Mando'a/Legends|''Mando'a'']], ''Dha Werda Verda'' meant "Warriors of the Shadow," a name the Taungs took for themselves during their legendary [[Zhell–Taung war|war]] with the [[Battalion/Legends|Battalions]] of [[Zhell/Legends|Zhell]]. On the ancient [[Planet/Legends|planet]] of [[Coruscant/Legends|Coruscant]], then known merely as Notron, the Taungs' and the Zhells' conflict was interrupted by the eruption of a great [[Volcano/Legends|volcano]], that [[Blanket/Legends|blanketed]] the [[Atmosphere/Legends|skies]] in ash and blotted out [[Coruscant Prime/Legends|the sun]]. The forces of the Zhell were devastated, and the Taungs—seeing the eruption as divine providence—named their warriors for the massive shadow of the ash cloud that gave them an advantage over their Zhell enemies. Though the Zhell later recovered and eventually managed to drive the Taungs from ancient Notron in the end, the war and the eruption were recounted by the Taungs in a poetic epic known only as the ''Dha Werda Verda'', an epic the rest of [[The galaxy/Legends|the galaxy's]] [[Historian/Legends|scholars]] would later study from Taung [[Roonstone|relics]] left behind on the planet [[Roon/Legends|Roon]] during their journey to a new home. Under the reign of their leader, [[Mandalore the First]], the Taungs settled a new world they deemed [[Mandalore/Legends|Mandalore]] in the great [[Chief/Legends|chieftain's]] honor, and again remade themselves as the [[Mandalorian/Legends|Mandalorians]] or ''Mando'ade''—sons and daughters of Mandalore. The Mandalorian Taungs went on to conquer a number of other worlds, and over [[Time/Legends|time]], the Taungs began to accept beings of various other races into their culture. All the while, their near constant crusades depleted the Mandalorians' Taung population, and by the time of [[Mandalore the Ultimate]]'s reign, the culture's Taung progenitors had become vastly eclipsed by the many other races that now called themselves Mandalorian. Into this atmosphere came a new iteration of the ''Dha Werda Verda'', a great war chant also known as the "'''Rage of the Shadow Warriors'''." This new chant was birthed by the Taungs' understanding that they were in decline, and that the multi-species Mandalorian culture would long outlast them, a plea of sorts that their contributions and place in history not be forgotten. The new chant was accompanied by a ritual dance, wherein performers would [[Drum/Legends|drum]] the rhythm of the ''Dha Werda Verda'' on the [[Armor/Legends|armored]] chest and back of those beside them, and was passed down through countless generations of Mandalorians, even being taught to the [[Clone trooper/Legends|clone soldiers]] of the [[Grand Army of the Republic/Legends|Grand Army of the Republic]] by their Mandalorian [[Cuy'val Dar|instructors]] as a way to honor their heritage as [[Cloning/Legends|clones]] of [[Mandalore (title)/Legends|Mandalore]] [[Jango Fett/Legends|Jango Fett]]. ==History== {{Quote|The outlines of what happened some 200,000 years ago are known to every schoolchild: The 13 nations that made up the Battalions of Zhell spent centuries clashing with the forces of the Taungs. During one of their skirmishes, a volcanic eruption destroyed the city of Zhell, shattering the Battalions' power. The assembled Taungs watched in awe as ash blotted out the sun and rained down upon them. Taking their opponents' destruction as a sign of divine favor, the Taungs christened themselves ''Dha Werda Verda'', the Shadow Warriors, and celebrated their victory in the epic poem of the same name.|[[Eschul Shaywa]], contributor for [[Imperial Center Today]]|}} [[File:TaungMartialArtsMaster-GAW.jpg|thumb|right|180px|The [[Taung/Legends|Taung]] species, immortalized in the epic poem]] On the ancient [[Core Worlds/Legends|Core World]] of [[Coruscant/Legends|Coruscant]],''[[The Essential Atlas]]'' in a [[Time/Legends|time]] when it was known only as Notron, the rival societies of the [[Taung/Legends|Taungs]] and the early [[Human/Legends|Human]] [[Zhell/Legends|Zhell]] rose to prominence and came into [[Zhell–Taung war|conflict]] with one another. The two peoples warred for [[Standard century/Legends|centuries]], in a struggle that came to a head following the [[Death/Legends|death]] of the Taung leader [[Doom of Ulmarah]]. Rallying behind [[Rexutu the Unconquerable|Rexutu]] the Unconquerable, the warriors of the Taungs gathered at [[High place|their fortress]] to battle the [[Battalion/Legends|Battalions]] of Zhell at dawn; the Taungs were fewer in number than the more populous Zhell, and expected the battle to end in their defeat. Before combat could begin, however, a large [[Volcano/Legends|volcano]] erupted, devastating the forces of the Zhell as well as their [[Great Zhell|capital city]]. The Taungs, a highly [[Mandalorian religion|religious]] society,{{InsiderCite|80|The History of the Mandalorians: Exploring the Evolution of a Mighty Race of Warriors}} saw the destruction of their foe as divine favor, and as an enormous cloud of volcanic ash blotted out [[Coruscant Prime/Legends|the sun]], the Taungs proclaimed themselves the ''Dha Werda Verda''—the "Warriors of the Shadow." Though the Zhell were eventually able to recover from the volcano's devastation, and by the war's end succeeded in defeating the Taungs, driving them from ancient Notron, the Taungs still celebrated and recorded their legendary victory that day in a poetic epic they entitled the ''Dha Werda Verda''. ===The poetic ''Dha Werda Verda''=== {{Quote|No matter what school, junior academy or crèche you belonged to, if you're Coruscanti you either memorized the strange syllables of these 10 verses for recitation or had a schoolmate who did.|Eschul Shaywa|{{SW|url=news/star-wars-the-essential-guide-to-warfare-authors-cut-part-2-ancient-coruscant|series=Star Wars: The Essential Guide to Warfare Author's Cut|text=Part 2 - Ancient Coruscant}}}} Authored by the Taungs, the ''Dha Werda Verda'' was an epic poem that told the tale of their victory over the Zhell in the shadow a volcanic ash cloud that devastated the early Humans. The ''Dha Werda Verda'' was written in the ancient [[Language/Legends|language]] of [[Notron Cant]], a dialect of notable linguistic subtleties. In total, the ''Dha Werda Verda'' encompassed more than seven hundred verses, and was divided into eleven chapters. The ninth chapter, comprised of ten verses, was known as "The Maker Comes to Unmake." {{ExcerptIU|"Booten wooten lanlock vootem / Al a sinkee dunken pooten / Achta werda verda roll / Poonka dunkee loten cho. Leeber soong whar tung tach picta / Manner manner migta richta / Schelecht varn toom-soing pa ho-grunten / Gersh ve dala funken mimpa / Droit! / To Gropen wettkampf Zunken! Betteltung seeck da mindy cooten / Parta blax dha scunken drassen. Manner manner, mitteltouse manner / Dha Dhazz jedoch / Land zu land offt letza / Unun nung. Manner manner / Durchsprung Nocha / Immer hauk gewordenspa / Zeeetoof en poof / Olaffka begonnenspah / Var var goopinski / von moglodite / Kortzva. Verto verto taplasko ta verto. Vom zoomenfest / Va va voomenfest / Kopocka locka hatta statan / Schel Tha noobin rest du common / Morbskurtz! Kaffee kaffee zum doom kaffee! / Ausbroll mobist manner mockah! / Ssstrung tartung tha stroong tartung! / Wo-cha nickschat hobbentrose. Jungclaus dha spricken / Impoot ga kunginchock! Kungach / Noplenkacht / Kungar Kungar / Ale Da Kungare!"|attrib=''Dha Werda Verda''[[Shadows of the Empire (soundtrack)|''Shadows of the Empire'' soundtrack]]}} Following their [[Exile (punishment)/Legends|exile]] from ancient Notron, the Taungs traveled through [[The galaxy/Legends|the galaxy]], eventually settling on the planet [[Roon/Legends|Roon]] in the [[Outer Rim Territories/Legends|Outer Rim]]. Though they later moved on to a new world, it was on Roon that the Taungs left behind a number of relics, including great [[Mythosaur axe/Legends|axes]], [[Sword/Legends|swords]], and a copy of the ''Dha Werda Verda'' poem encoded in Notron Cant on a crystalline [[roonstone]]. These precious [[Gemstone/Legends|gems]] could store vast amounts of data, and kept the ''Dha Werda Verda'' preserved for future generations.{{DB|character|mungobaobab|Baobab, Mungo}} Millennia later, in the [[Standard year/Legends|year]] [[15 BBY/Legends|15 BBY]],{{PolyhedronCite|170|Star Wars Droids: The Adventures of R2-D2 and C-3PO}} the roonstone encoded with the ''Dha Werda Verda'' was discovered by the explorer [[Mungo Baobab]], who delivered the relic to the [[Baobab Archives]] on the planet [[Manda/Legends|Manda]] for study.''[[The New Essential Guide to Characters]]'' The copy of the ''Dha Werda Verda'' on Manda was the best preserved record of the epic poem, but not the only one: over the millennia that followed the Taungs' ash-shrouded victory, [[Historian/Legends|historians]] and scholars studied partial records of the ''Dha Werda Verda'' from archaeological finds, despite the fact that the subtleties of Notron Cant continued to defy complete translation. Over time, the most [[Galaxy/Legends|galactically]] well known portion of the ''Dha Werda Verda'' became "The Maker Comes to Unmake," the ten verses from the epic's ninth chapter. By the [[Galactic Empire/Legends|Galactic Empire's]] [[Declaration of a New Order|rise]] to power, [[Coruscanti/Legends|Coruscanti]] school children were taught the Notron Cant verses of "The Maker Comes to Unmake" as part of their education, and artists, inspired by the tale of the ancient Taungs, created various works based on the ''Dha Werda Verda''. [[Mesh Burzon]] of the [[University of Byblos/Legends|University of Byblos]] considered the Human veneration of the ''Dha Werda Verda'' an odd social phenomenon, as it chronicles the near destruction of the Human Zhell by the Taungs. [[Hu Jibwe]], a [[Army/Legends|military]] history scholar at the [[Salmagodro Grand Academy]], believed that the Taungs and Zhell forces were significantly more technologically advanced than the ''Dha Werda Verda'' poetically portrays, but nonetheless found the imagery of the poem stirring. Eventually, the Baobab Archives succeeded in translating the ninth chapter of the ''Dha Werda Verda'' from Notron Cant into [[Galactic Basic Standard/Legends|Galactic Basic Standard]]. {{Excerpt|1=
And so upon his [[Cremation/Legends|pyre]] burned the Doom of Ulmarah, and the warrior bands stood as ragged as bandits, in zigzag lines of mourning. With the dawn the flat-faced Zhell would come, cackling and howling, oozing mirth and tricks, and find the shade of the Doom departed and the Taungs unprotected. And so with the dawn would our woe be revealed. Our once-bright armaments would become stacked [[Burial/Legends|grave]] goods, trophies for Zhell [[Youngling/Legends|children]]. Our flesh would become smoke given to uncaring gods, and the sky would forget our names. With death upon him Rexutu the Unconquerable prepared to be stripped of all by his enemy, but vowed that his honor would be the last to be torn away. And so the Unconquerable gathered his kinsmen and his oath girdlings alike. They polished their fearsome [[Armor/Legends|helms]], that they might flash even in the weak [[Star/Legends|sun]] of Notron. They rewrapped the [[Hilt/Legends|hilts]] of their weapons and pounded straight the shafts, that they might slake their thirst in Zhell [[Blood/Legends|ichor]] a final time. Assembled they ascended, in taut Taung lines, to the high place where the [[Olhak the Reaver|Reaver]] had staked his standard before it was cast down into the mire. They gazed out over the gathering places and walking ways of Great Zhell where they scaled peak and cradled valley, the line of lights ordering the night. They unfurled the Taung banner, reversed, a reckless thing snapping in the dark, awaiting Zhell [[Eye/Legends|eyes]]. And they performed ceremonies of leave-taking, for now they had died to the world and must be remade among the stars. When the dawn came the Zhell awakened and saw the Taungs upon the high place and were afraid, for the morning light caught the glint of helms and weapons and created phantom warriors, made of dazzle and distance. But the cleverest of them were not deceived, and saw how few we were. And so they assembled without haste, merry in mockery, and prepared to march. And in the high place we awaited death. But then came a shaking of the ground, and the sun's wan light was eclipsed by a bright and terrible [[Fire/Legends|fire]] that exploded from the rock. The patterns of Great Zhell shivered and broke. And after this came darkness, as the very air turned to [[Color/Legends|black]] ash. The Zhell fell on their faces in terror, and from the high place we ran in haste to meet them, and we were cloaked in shadow. The Maker had come to unmake, and the Taungs would be His instruments.
|source=''[[The Essential Guide to Warfare]]''|attrib=[[Jason Fry]] and [[Paul R. Urquhart]]|copyright=true}} ===The ''Dha Werda Verda'' reinvented=== [[File:Mand-Group_small.jpg|thumb|left|210px|The [[Mandalorian/Legends|Mandalorians]], the cultural descendants of the Taung]] {{Quote|It's my belief that 'Rage of the Shadow Warriors' dates from the reign of Mandalore the Ultimate, when the Taungs knew they were being eclipsed. I've always thought it a poignant work—a plea that the Taungs not be forgotten by the newborn culture they knew would outlive them.|[[Hu Jibwe]], military history scholar|}} After departing Roon, the Taungs found a new world to call home. Naming the planet [[Mandalore/Legends|Mandalore]] in honor of their leader, [[Mandalore the First]], the Taungs, in turn, rechristened themselves the [[Mandalorian/Legends|Mandalorians]] or ''Mando'ade'' in their tongue—"sons and daughters of Mandalore." As the Taungs changed, so too did their culture and language: instead of Notron Cant, the Taungs had begun to speak an archaic precursor to the Mandalorian language of [[Mando'a/Legends|''Mando'a'']],''[[Galaxy at War]]'' and over time, began to accept beings of various other [[Species/Legends|races]] into their clans as equals under the ''[[Resol'nare]]'', the six basic tenets of the Mandalorian culture.{{InsiderCite|86|The Mandalorians: People and Culture}} However, the Mandalorian Taungs had become [[Mandalorian Crusaders|zealous conquerors]], engaging in holy crusades against other peoples such as the [[Fenelar]] and [[Basiliskan]]s in service first to their [[God/Legends|god]] [[Kad Ha'rangir]], then to the very divinity of war itself. Even as the Mandalorians swelled in number, the Taungs were in decline, depleted by their many wars. By the reign of [[Mandalore the Ultimate]], the Taungs had come to realize that they were being eclipsed by the influx of non-Taung Mandalorians into the culture they had birthed. It was this atmosphere that led to the creation of a new iteration of the ''Dha Werda Verda'', one born of the Taungs' desire to be remembered by the Mandalorian generations that would long outlast them. Also known as the "Rage of the Shadow Warriors," the new ''Dha Werda Verda'' was a ritual battle chant, sung in the Mandalorian language of ''Mando'a'' instead of Notron Cant, with all new lyrical content, none of which was shared with the original ''Dha Werda Verda'' poem. It recalled the volcanic ash that had granted the Taungs their legendary victory over the Zhell, and spoke to the Mandalorians' Taung heritage, as well as the Taungs' place as the founders of Mandalore and its culture.''[[The Bounty Hunter Code: From the Files of Boba Fett]]''
{| {{Prettytable}} ! ''Mando'a''||Basic |- |colspan="1"|''Taung sa rang broka Mando'ade ka'rta.
Dha Werda Verda a'den tratu,
Manda'yaim kandosii adu.
Duum motir ca'tra nau tracinya.
Gra'tua cuun hett su dralshy'a.

Kom'rk tsad droten troch nyn ures adenn.
Dha Werda Verda a'den tratu,
Manda'yaim kandosii adu.
Duum motir ca'tra nau tracinya.
Gra'tua cuun hett su dralshy'a.''||colspan="1"|The ash of the Taung beats strong within the Mandalorians' hearts.
We are the rage of The Warriors of the Shadow,
The first noble sons of Mandalore.
Let all those who stand before us light the night sky in flame.
Our vengeance burns brighter still.

The gauntlet of Mandalore strikes without mercy.
We are the rage of The Warriors of the Shadow,
The first noble sons of Mandalore.
Let all those who stand before us light the night sky in flame.
Our vengeance burns brighter still. |}
The new "Rage of the Shadow Warriors" iteration of the ''Dha Werda Verda'' was often accompanied by a ritual dance. While singing the lyrics, performers of the ''Dha Werda Verda'' would first [[Drum/Legends|drum]] the layered rhythms of the [[Music/Legends|music]] on their own bodies with their [[Limb/Legends|fists]], before turning to beat the complex tempo on the body of the individual next to them, specifically striking the chest and back. The ''Dha Werda Verda'' dance required stamina, timing, and trust in the individuals that surrounded a performer; the rhythmic strikes were imbued with notable force, and to turn too quickly or too late was to risk sustaining an injuring blow, especially to the head or face. "Rage of the Shadow Warriors" was typically done while wearing [[Mandalorian armor/Legends|armor]], which softened blows and shielded the performer's body, but was also performed in plain [[Clothing/Legends|clothes]] on occasion. It was believed that engaging in the ''Dha Werda Verda'' and performing the complex rhythms with a group of comrades helped to sharpen the mind and taught cooperative coordination. A number of [[Soldier/Legends|soldiers]]—both Mandalorians and outsiders alike—found the ''Dha Werda Verda'' an exciting exercise, and felt that they drew strength and courage from the act of performing the ritual dance. To outsiders, it was a fearsome display of discipline and reflex that served to warn an enemy of the power of the force that opposed them. The ''Dha Werda Verda''{{'s}} "Rage of the Shadow Warriors" incarnation proved popular to the Mandalorian people and culture: Mandalorian warriors chanted the ''Dha Werda Verda'' in honor of the Taungs who came before, and the ritual dance was performed by Mandalorians for thousands of years after Mandalore the Ultimate's reign came to an [[Battle of Malachor V|end]]. In a manifesto [[Author/Legends|authored]] by the [[Death Watch/Legends|Death Watch]] founder [[Tor Vizsla]], entitled ''[[Ba'jurne Kyr'tsad Mando'ad]]'', Vizsla wrote on the cultural importance of the ''Dha Werda Verda'' to the Mandalorian warrior clans, and discussed the idea that every time the "Rage of the Shadow Warriors" was chanted, the Mandalorians were paying homage to their culture's Taung progenitors. The military history scholar Hu Jibwe studied the Mandalorian ''Dha Werda Verda'', determining that the "Rage of the Shadow Warriors" originated more recently than the original poem written in Notron Cant, cross-referencing the Mandalorian war chant with the ''Dha Werda Verda''-encoded roonstone housed in the Baobab Archives on Manda. When writing her piece for [[Imperial Center Today]] entitled "[[A LONG TIME AGO...]]," [[Eschul Shaywa]] heavily researched both incarnations of the ''Dha Werda Verda'', speaking with several ancient history scholars and even attending a performance of the ritual dance. Shaywa found the Mandalorian battle chant mesmerizing. ====Adoption by the Grand Army==== {{Quote|I've seen a few squads do it. It came via Skirata, I hear.''"
"''Yes. He taught all the commandos to live up to their Mandalorian heritage. You know—customs, language, ideals. It's very weird. It's like they have a compulsion to do it.''"
"''Yes, we do. It's very stirring.''"
"''I'm sorry. That was rude of me.''"
"''No problem, General. It certainly wasn't part of our trooper training on Kamino. It gets passed on from man to man now.|[[Clone Commander/Legends|Clone Commander]] [[Gett]] and [[Jedi General/Legends|Jedi General]] [[Etain Tur-Mukan]], watching [[Clone trooper/Legends|clone troopers]] and [[Clone commando/Legends|commandos]] perform the ''Dha Werda Verda''|}} [[File:Jango_Fett_and_Kal_Skirata.jpg|thumb|right|200px|The Mandalorian ''Cuy'val Dar'' passed the tradition of the ''Dha Werda Verda'' to the Grand Army's clone soldiers]] In [[32 BBY/Legends|32 BBY]],''[[The New Essential Chronology]]'' [[Mandalore (title)/Legends|Mandalore]]''[[Order 66: A Republic Commando Novel]]'' [[Jango Fett/Legends|Jango Fett]] was recruited to be the [[DNA/Legends|genetic]] [[Clone template/Legends|template]] for an army of [[Cloning/Legends|clone]] soldiers that would serve the [[Galactic Republic/Legends|Galactic Republic]]. In order to train the [[Grand Army of the Republic/Legends|Grand Army of the Republic's]] [[Special forces/Legends|special forces]], Fett recruited a group of one hundred training [[Sergeant/Legends|sergeants]] known as the ''[[Cuy'val Dar]]'', seventy-five of whom were of Mandalorian origin. These Mandalorian instructors brought a number of Mandalorian traditions to the Grand Army, with the veteran Mandalorian soldier [[Kal Skirata]] introducing the ''Dha Werda Verda'' dance to the [[Clone commando/Legends|clone commandos]] that trained under him, including the [[Null-class Advanced Recon Commando|''Null''-class Advanced Recon Commandos]]. However, like a number of other Mandalorian songs the clones learned in training, such as ''[[Gra'tua Cuun]]'', ''[[Ka'rta Tor]]'',[[Star Wars: Republic Commando (soundtrack)|''Star Wars: Republic Commando'' soundtrack]] and ''[[Vode An]]''—which became the Grand Army's marching anthem—the "Rage of the Shadow Warrior" lyrics learned by the Grand Army's clone soldiers had been altered to remove its connection to Mandalorian culture. In the ''Dha Werda Verda'' tought to the clones, references to Mandalore were replaced with Coruscant, now the [[Capital/Legends|capital]] world of the Galactic Republic, and references to the Mandalorians were replaced with [[Jedi/Legends|Jedi]], who would be serving as [[General/Legends|generals]] in command of the army.
{| {{Prettytable}} ! ''Mando'a''||Basic |- |colspan="1"|''Taung sa rang broka jetiise ka'rta.
Dha Werda Verda a'den tratu,
Coruscanta kandosii adu.
Duum motir ca'tra nau tracinya.
Gra'tua cuun hett su dralshy'a.

Kom'rk tsad droten troch nyn ures adenn.
Dha Werda Verda a'den tratu,
Coruscanta kandosii adu.
Duum motir ca'tra nau tracinya.
Gra'tua cuun hett su dralshy'a.''||colspan="1"|The ash of the Taung beats strong within the Jedi's heart.
We are the rage of The Warriors of the Shadow,
The first noble sons of Coruscant.
Let all those who stand before us light the night sky in flame.
Our vengeance burns brighter still.

The gauntlet of Coruscant strikes without mercy.
We are the rage of The Warriors of the Shadow,
The first noble sons of Coruscant.
Let all those who stand before us light the night sky in flame.
Our vengeance burns brighter still. |}
In [[22 BBY/Legends|22 BBY]], the Grand Army was deployed against the [[Separatist Droid Army/Legends|droid army]] of the [[Confederacy of Independent Systems/Legends|Confederacy of Independent Systems]] following the [[First Battle of Geonosis/Legends|outbreak]] of the [[Clone Wars/Legends|Clone Wars]] on [[Geonosis/Legends|Geonosis]]. After the battle on Geonosis came to a close, numerous clone commandos that had been involved in the fight were taken to the [[Fleet Ops|Fleet Support]] station on [[Ord Mantell/Legends|Ord Mantell]]. While at the [[Barracks/Legends|barracks]] there, commando [[Niner Skirata|Niner]] sang a chorus of the "Rage of the Shadow Warriors" as a means to comfort himself over the loss of the rest of [[Sev (Niner's squad)|his squad]]. He was joined shortly after by his peer, [[Fi Skirata|Fi]], who lamented the demise of his [[Teroch Squad|own squad]] in the battle.''[[Republic Commando: Hard Contact]]'' As the war progressed, [[Jedi Knight/Legends|Jedi Knight]] [[Bardan Jusik]]'s time around the Grand Army's clone commandos led him develop an interest in the ''Dha Werda Verda'', finding the ritual dance an inspiring sight that gave him courage just by watching it, and he learned to perform it with the men under his command. Although the ''Dha Werda Verda'' had only been taught to the clone commandos in the charge of the Mandalorian ''Cuy'val Dar'' sergeants on their [[Homeworld/Legends|homeworld]] of [[Kamino/Legends|Kamino]], as the war went on, bits of Mandalorian culture including the ''Dha Werda Verda'' began to filter through the larger army, spread [[Gender/Legends|man]] to man. Near the end of the first year of the war, after a rescue of the commando squads [[Delta Squad/Legends|Delta]] and [[Omega Squad|Omega]] brought them aboard [[Acclamator-class transgalactic military assault ship/Legends|RAS]] ''[[Fearless]]'' alongside the [[41st Stormtrooper Legion|41st Elite Legion's]] [[Sarlacc Battalions]]—themselves fresh from an [[Skirmish at Dinlo|extraction]] from [[Dinlo]]—the commandos taught their [[Clone trooper/Legends|clone trooper]] comrades how to perform the ''Dha Werda Verda''. While [[Jedi General/Legends|Jedi General]] [[Etain Tur-Mukan]] watched with [[Clone Commander/Legends|Clone Commander]] [[Gett]], the commandos of both Omega and Delta Squads led a performance of the ''Dha Werda Verda'' from the front of an assembly of close to fifty soldiers of the 41st Elite. [[Commander/Legends|Commander]] Gett [[Holorecorder/Legends|recorded]] a portion of the rare performance, before joining in the ritual dance with General Tur-Mukan's permission. Tur-Mukan found the obvious word replacements in the lyrics and their effect of stripping the chant of its Mandalorian heritage a distasteful decision that further distanced the clones from what she saw as their inherited culture. Not long after, while taking part in a [[Terrorist/Legends|terrorist]] [[Mission to Coruscant (Clone Wars)|takedown]] on Coruscant alongside the commandos of Delta and Omega Squads, the ''Null''-class [[Advanced Recon Commando/Legends|ARC troopers]] [[Ordo Skirata|Ordo]] and [[Mereel Skirata|Mereel]], and Jedi Generals Etain Tur-Mukan and Bardan Jusik, the former ''Cuy'val Dar'' sergeant Kal Skirata wished he could engage in a round of the ''Dha Werda Verda'' to mentally bolster himself for combat. However, the low profile necessitated by the mission kept him from indulging. After the anti-terrorist operation came to a successful conclusion, all parties involved—clone, Jedi, and the Mandalorian Skirata—were invited to the [[Coruscant Security Force Staff and Social Club|CSF Social Club]] by the [[Officer/Legends|officers]] of the [[Coruscant Security Force/Legends|Coruscant Security Force]]. While in attendance, the commando by the name of Fi got a performance of the ''Dha Werda Verda'' going for the benefit of the assembled [[Law enforcement agency/Legends|police]] officers: the commandos and ARC troopers took part in the chant and dance while wearing their armor, though Skirata did so wearing only his civilian clothing. During the performance, the [[Alpha-class Advanced Recon Commando|''Alpha''-class ARC trooper]] [[Maze (clone trooper)|Maze]] used the blows of the ''Dha Werda Verda'' as an excuse punch his ''Null'' ARC contemporary, [[Clone Captain/Legends|Captain]] Ordo, in return for a previous strike Ordo had inflicted upon Maze; when the ''Dha Werda Verda'' came to an end, both soldiers shook hands, leaving their grievances settled and in the past. The elderly Skirata, having gone without armor during the ritual, emerged from the ''Dha Werda Verda'' performance sore and beginning to bruise, but exhilarated. ==Behind the scenes== {{Quote|Ben Burtt's transcript of the text to Dha Werda Verda helped to shape its form. The epic poem's violent and sometimes romantic images fit perfectly with the musical structure of this piece. I utilized the text in sections, applying the portion most applicable to the scene being scored. However, my translations were quite vague, so sometimes the meaning might have gotten a bit skewed.|[[Joel McNeely]]|}} Written by [[Ben Burtt]], the ''Dha Werda Verda'' first entered ''[[Star Wars]]'' [[canon]] with the release of the [[Shadows of the Empire (soundtrack)|''Shadows of the Empire'' soundtrack]] on [[April 23]], [[1996]], and was named in the soundtrack's liner notes. Burtt, who has developed numerous alien languages for the ''Star Wars'' franchise, was asked by the project's composer [[Joel McNeely]] to write a poem that could then be sung by a chorus for the soundtrack, specifically requesting that the language used be [[:Wikipedia:Germanic languages|Germanic]] in style, with hard, harsh, guttural-sounding syllables. Burtt complied, and McNeely then used pieces of the poem in his compositions, though they were placed out of context. The poem—the in-universe discovery of which Burtt ties into characters and events he developed for the [[Star Wars: Droids: The Adventures of R2-D2 and C-3PO|''Star Wars: Droids'']] animated series of the mid-[[1980]]s—details the ancient conflict between the Battalions of Zhell and the Taungs. According to the poem, the battle of the two peoples was interrupted by a volcanic eruption which wiped out the Zhell, and the site of the battle eventually became [[Galactic City/Legends|Imperial City]] millennia later. Nearly a decade later, the ''[[Star Wars: Republic Commando]]'' [[Star Wars video games|video game]] and its accompanying [[Star Wars: Republic Commando (soundtrack)|soundtrack]] were released on [[March 22]], [[2005]].''[[Star Wars: Republic Commando]]'' The soundtrack, composed by [[LucasArts]] music editor and composer [[Jesse Harlin]], featured new audio that referred to the ''Dha Werda Verda'' in the thirteenth track entitled ''Rage of the Shadow Warriors''. These lyrics, however, differed from the ''Dha Werda Verda'' written years prior by Burtt. ''Star Wars'' author [[Karen Traviss]] incorporated Harlin's lyrics from the ''Republic Commando'' soundtrack into the foundation of ''Mando'a'', the constructed Mandalorian language she developed for her work with the Mandalorian people,{{WebCite|url=http://www.karentraviss.com/page20/page26/index.html|text=Mando'a|author=Karen Traviss|work=[http://www.karentraviss.com Karen Traviss]|archivedate=20110925083955}} [[Retroactive continuity|retroactively]] creating a link between the two versions of the ''Dha Werda Verda''. In her ''[[Republic Commando]]'' novel series, Traviss featured the lyrics of the "Rage of the Shadow Warriors" first in [[Republic Commando: Hard Contact|''Hard Contact'']], then in its sequel, [[Republic Commando: Triple Zero|''Triple Zero'']]. In ''Triple Zero'', Traviss detailed an additional ritual war dance, highly reminiscent of the traditional [[Wikipedia:Māori culture|Māori]] [[Wikipedia:Haka|''haka'']];[[:Wikipedia:Haka|The Haka on Wikipedia]] [[Temuera Morrison]], the actor who portrays Mandalorian warrior Jango Fett and his clones in the ''Star Wars'' films, is of Māori heritage,{{WebCite|url=http://www.nzherald.co.nz/nz/news/article.cfm|text=Temeura Morrison's ancestry|nobackup=1}} and has been known to demonstrate the haka for fans at conventions.{{YouTube|video=8v9YfO9yn00|text=Temuera Morrison performs the haka|channel=@NerdReactor|name=Nerd Reactor}} ''[[The Essential Guide to Warfare]]'', a ''Star Wars'' reference book co-authored by [[Jason Fry]] and [[Paul R. Urquhart]] and published [[April 3]], [[2012]], was the first source to feature a canonical translation for the original ''Dha Werda Verda'' poem. In a section Fry likened to the style of the [[:Wikipedia:Epic of Gilgamesh|epic of Gilgamesh]],{{JCFCite|url=posts/30941674|board=Literature|thread=The Essential Guide to Warfare by Jason Fry and a pseudonymous Scotsman|user=[[Jason Fry|jasonfry]]|date=2010-05-19|time=2:04 PM|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20160415220442/http://boards.theforce.net/threads/the-essential-guide-to-warfare-by-jason-fry-and-a-pseudonymous-scotsman.30937840/page-3}} the in-universe excerpt of the ''Dha Werda Verda'' chronicled the lead up to the climactic, ash-shrouded battle between the Zhell and Taungs. The ''Guide to Warfare'' was originally going to have a more extensive history on the ''Dha Werda Verda'', but it was cut for length, only to resurface on [[November 4]], [[2013]], with the second installment of Jason Fry's official [[Star Wars Blog|''Star Wars'' Blog]] series, [[Star Wars: The Essential Guide to Warfare Author's Cut|''The Essential Guide to Warfare'' Author's Cut]], entitled "Ancient Coruscant." The blog entry finally outlined the canonical connection between the original poetic ''Dha Werda Verda'' as written by Ben Burtt with the later "Rage of the Shadow Warriors" track composed by Jesse Harlin, as two works with a shared name and inspiration in Taung history. The blog also identified the language of the poetic ''Dha Werda Verda'', unnamed for more than a decade, as Notron Cant. ==Appearances== *''[[Star Wars: The Old Republic]]'' {{Codex}} *''[[Star Wars: The Old Republic: Knights of the Fallen Empire]]'' *''[[Star Wars: The Old Republic: Knights of the Eternal Throne]]'' *''[[Star Wars: Republic Commando]]'' {{1st}} {{C|as the "Rage of the Shadow Warriors"}} *''[[Republic Commando: Hard Contact]]'' *''[[Republic Commando: Triple Zero]]'' *{{InsiderCite|70|Republic HoloNet News Core Edition 14:9:04}} ==Sources== *[[Shadows of the Empire (soundtrack)|''Shadows of the Empire'' soundtrack]] {{C|First appearance of the poetic ''Dha Werda Verda''}} {{Ln}} {{1stID|''Dha Werda Verda''}} *{{GalaxyCite|7|Around the Galaxy}} *{{InsiderCite|29|Scoring the Trilogy|Scoring the Trilogy: ''Shadows of the Empire''}} {{Imo}} *{{GalaxyCite|9|Galactic Bazaar|Galactic Bazaar: Collectors' Comlink}} *''[[Secrets of the Sisar Run]]'' *''[[Star Wars Encyclopedia]]'' *''[[C-3PO: Tales of the Golden Droid]]'' *[[The Essential Chronology (real-world book)|''The Essential Chronology'']] *''[[A Guide to the Star Wars Universe, Third Edition, Revised and Expanded]]'' *''[[Coruscant and the Core Worlds]]'' *[[Star Wars: Republic Commando (soundtrack)|''Star Wars: Republic Commando'' soundtrack]] *''[[The New Essential Chronology]]'' *{{LucasArts|url=games/swrepubliccommando/downloads/mp3s/Lyrics.pdf|text="Star Wars: Republic Commando: English Translation of Ancient Mandalorian Text"}} *''[[The Complete Star Wars Encyclopedia]]'' *''[[The Essential Atlas]]'' *''[[The Essential Guide to Warfare]]'' *{{SW|url=news/the-droids-re-animated-part-2|series=The Droids Re-Animated|text=Part 2|nolive=1}} *''[[The Bounty Hunter Code: From the Files of Boba Fett]]'' *{{SW|url=news/star-wars-the-essential-guide-to-warfare-authors-cut-part-2-ancient-coruscant|series=Star Wars: The Essential Guide to Warfare Author's Cut|text=Part 2 - Ancient Coruscant}} ==Notes and references== {{ScrollBox|height=300px|content= {{Reflist}} }} {{Interlang |cs=Dha Werda Verda |de=Dha Werda Verda |es=Dha Werda Verda |nl=Dha Werda Verda }} [[Category:Dance]] [[Category:Mandalorian chants and songs]] [[Category:Poems]]
